notes: Dialogic reported >2 million installed ports globally as of 1997.
Dialogic had 2,000+ application partners successfully deployed on its products.
Dialogic products deployed in 74 countries per the study.
CT Media was the only NT-based enterprise S.100 CT platform available in 1997 per Aberdeen.
BOSTON S.100 API support not expected until 1998 per Aberdeen comparison table.
CT Access did not support S.100, TAPI, or other industry standard APIs.
MediaPath did not support S.100 API; restricted to Mitel switching.

metric_name: CT Media resource management for multiple apps
notes: Only CT Media provided resource management enabling multiple vendor apps to share single CT server hardware.
CT Media operated in true client-server environment unlike all competitors evaluated.
Aberdeen rated CT Media's abstraction from hardware as 'Complete'; competitors rated Device-level or None.
Customer demand for S.100 compliance was the single most important driver for CT developer migration to CT Media.

metric_value: Business decision not just technology choice
notes: Aberdeen framed CT platform choice as a business decision with long-term strategic implications.

metric_name: CT Media as catalyst for broader CT market
metric_value: CT Media will expand the CT market
notes: Aberdeen predicted CT Media would create a multiplier effect by clarifying developer/integrator/reseller roles.
Dialogic positioned as standards champion; open platform strategy as long-term CT market development play.
Dialogic was founding member of ECTF; drove S.100, H.100, S.300 standards development.

metric_name: CT Media as de facto open CT platform standard
metric_value: Will become the standard open CT middleware
notes: Aberdeen predicted CT Media would become the de facto standard for enterprise open CT server development.

metric_name: ECTF S.100 adoption by CT industry
metric_value: S.100 will prevail as CT media API standard
notes: Aberdeen predicted S.100 compliance would become mandatory; industry was moving toward it.

metric_value: CT will transition to broader market via open standards
notes: Aberdeen predicted open standards would catalyze CT market expansion, moving it from craft to mass market.
Aberdeen noted BOSTON promised S.100 in 1998. Brooktrout did expand VoIP but was acquired before major impact.
Intel acquired Dialogic in 1999 for approximately $780 million, validating Aberdeen's assessment of CT infrastructure value.

metric_name: CT Media succeeded by VoIP/SIP standards
notes: The ECTF framework (S.100, H.100) was ultimately superseded by VoIP/SIP standards; CT Media was not the long-term standard.

metric_value: Open standards CT did expand the market
notes: The CT/telecom software market did expand dramatically via open standards, though through VoIP rather than ECTF.
Brooktrout was acquired by EAS Group in 2005, merged into Cantata Technology, then acquired by Dialogic Group in 2007.
ECTF S.300 and S.400 were proposed standards that never achieved significant market adoption; VoIP architecture replaced the framework.
TAPI established as de facto standard for call control in Windows CT environment per Aberdeen.
